text:
lume's design names three replaceability contracts (scope.md / constraints.md):
Tracking, Review, and Practice. This workstream is about the *Tracking*
contract - the persistence of workstream/project-management state (objective,
iterations, plan, decisions, retro, discovery; the lifecycle). Its v1
implementation is the local-filesystem `Repository` class; scope.md's example
pair is files -> GitHub Issues / Jira / other PM tools.

Today only the filesystem implementation exists, so the design-for-
replaceability claim is proven by tests and design intent, not by lived use
(logged as a state-as-data carry-forward). `Repository` is also the
implementation name, not the contract name, and it conflates 'the PM-state
store' with 'the one store' - other aspects of lume (Review, Practice, and
future concerns like inference/config) are separate contracts that may live in
different stores.

This workstream builds a real second backing for the Tracking contract and runs
lume against it, so replaceability is demonstrated, not asserted. Where the
contract leaks (assumptions only the filesystem backing satisfies - e.g. engine
code that touches files around the seam), the leak is fixed or recorded. The
filesystem backing remains the default; the second backing is selectable.

Done-when: a second Tracking backing exists and is exercised by lume's verbs
(at minimum: resolve/list workstreams, load + save state, the lifecycle
transitions); the backing is selected through a documented contract rather than
by editing engine code; lume's existing behaviour on the filesystem backing is
unchanged (suite still green); and any contract leaks surfaced are resolved or
recorded as decisions. Open for discovery/planning: the concrete second backing
(real service vs in-process proof), and whether to rename `Repository` to name
the Tracking contract it implements.
title: Swappable Backing (Tracking contract)
